text: The Nyingma scholar Anne Klein, in her comparative study 'Unbounded Wholeness,' traces how the dzogchen concept of 'ground' — the primordial open expanse in which both samsara and nirvana arise — challenges standard Western notions of subjectivity. The ground is not a self that experiences; it has no center to protect, no story to maintain. This is why, Klein argues, resting in open presence feels threatening to the ordinary ego: it dissolves the very vantage point from which one normally evaluates experience. The felt sense of 'I am meditating' is itself a contraction away from rigpa, a subtle grasping that reinstates the observer and its objects.
question: According to the passage, why does resting in open presence feel threatening to the ordinary ego?

options.text: It requires confronting memories and emotions that the ego usually suppresses
It produces physical sensations of dissolution that the nervous system resists
It dissolves the vantage point from which the ego normally evaluates experience
It demands complete physical stillness that the body finds uncomfortable
